result:
React re-renders a child whenever the parent renders, by default. Props equality doesn't matter — React doesn't skip the render just because props look the same.

To stop it:

1. **`React.memo`** — wrap the child. It shallow-compares props and skips re-rendering when they're unchanged.
   ```jsx
   export default React.memo(Child);
   ```

2. **Stabilize props** — `memo` only helps if props are referentially stable. Inline objects/arrays/functions break it:
   ```jsx
   // bad: new object every render
   <Child config={{ x: 1 }} onClick={() => ...} />

   // good
   const config = useMemo(() => ({ x: 1 }), []);
   const onClick = useCallback(() => ..., []);
   <Child config={config} onClick={onClick} />
   ```

3. **Move state down** — if only part of the parent needs the state, push it into a smaller component so the rest doesn't re-render.

4. **Children as props** — content passed via `children` doesn't re-render when the parent's state changes, because it was created by the grandparent.

Most common cause: inline object/function props defeating `memo`. Check those first.

To stop it:

1. **`React.memo`** — wrap the child. It shallow-compares props and skips re-rendering when they're unchanged.

2. **Stabilize props** — `memo` only helps if props are referentially stable. Inline objects/arrays/functions break it:
   ```jsx

   const onClick = useCallback(() => ..., []);
   <Child config={config} onClick={onClick} />
   ```

3. **Move state down** — if only part of the parent needs the state, push it into a smaller component so the rest doesn't re-render.

4. **Children as props** — content passed via `children` doesn't re-render when the parent's state changes, because it was created by the grandparent.

Most common cause: inline object/function props defeating `memo`. Check those first.
